title: Global mapping of urban nature-based solutions for climate change adaptation
methods: systematic mapping (Collaboration for Environmental Evidence protocol)
data_sources: World Bank NbS for Disaster Risk Reduction portfolio
Urban Climate Change Research Network Case Study Docking Station
OpenStreetMap (city base maps, CC BY SA)

data_scope.notes: Grey-literature sources (funder/project databases, not peer-reviewed). Exact intervention dates not uniformly reported in main text. No satellite or gridded population data used.

core_conclusion: A systematic global mapping of 216 urban NbS interventions across 130 cities in 55 countries finds that current urban NbS practice is largely incremental and reformistic — not transformative. Practice is heavily biased toward the Global North (63% in Europe) and narrowly addresses climate–biodiversity–society (CBS) challenges, particularly underrepresenting social justice dimensions (recognition and distributional equity), private-sector engagement, and the climate hazards (drought, coastal) most severe in the Global South. Fulfilling NbS transformative potential requires expansion in geography, governance models, and social equity frameworks.
why_it_matters: First systematic cross-regional empirical database of implemented urban NbS (not modeled); exposes a structural North–South knowledge gap; links governance deficiencies (procedural-only justice, constrained public budgets, no legally binding planning) to transformative failure; identifies 'bright spot' cities for replication; provides an open, Zenodo-archived dataset.
why_nature: Cross-cutting CBS nexus framing aligns with Nature Sustainability's mandate; global scope with governance-level granularity; challenges dominant NbS optimism with evidence-based critique; fully open and reproducible (protocol + coding framework + full dataset archived).
added_value_to_my_research: Apply R2SFCA/2SFCA to the 130-city NbS database to measure who actually reaches NbS co-benefits — linking governance coding to spatial equity outcomes
Use ML-assisted NLP + remote sensing (Sentinel NDVI) to surface under-reported Global South NbS interventions and audit the paper's North bias
Apply MGWR/spatial DiD to model green gentrification risk as a spatial spillover from NbS locations using property-price and census panel data
Formalize a composite CBS-nexus index as a multi-objective facility-location target, extending my EV charging efficiency-vs-equity framework to urban NbS siting
Apply network community detection to the city–funding–actor graph to reveal knowledge-transfer pathways and identify whether 'bright spot' cities are network hubs

related.title: Biodiversity-rich recreational areas near cities as a nature-based mental health solution
related.relation: NbS co-benefit accessibility (mental health) — applies spatial accessibility logic to nature-based resources, extending the paper's co-benefit analysis

related.title: Systematic global stocktake of over 50,000 urban climate change studies
related.relation: ML-assisted systematic evidence synthesis at scale — provides the methodological blueprint for expanding the paper's 9-database grey-literature approach to a broader, ML-classified corpus

related.title: Green gentrification in European and North American cities
related.relation: Empirical evidence for the distributional justice risk the paper codes but cannot quantify — provides causal evidence linking green infrastructure to displacement

related.title: Urban cooling and energy-saving effects of nature-based solutions across types and scales
related.relation: Quantifies NbS co-benefits (cooling, energy saving) at comparable scale — complements the paper's governance coding with outcome-side evidence for multi-objective optimization

related.title: Unlocking urban climate adaptation imaginaries
related.relation: Studies local knowledge diversity in urban climate adaptation — directly relevant to the paper's call for context-sensitive, locally grounded NbS knowledge transfer

related.title: How NGOs reshape urban green space justice in Beijing
related.relation: Examines non-governmental actor roles in urban green space equity — complements the paper's actor-type analysis (finding that household and NGO actors are under-represented in NbS governance)

figures.caption: Geographical distribution of cities with mapped NbS for climate change adaptation — strong Europe-centric bias; grey countries have no interventions in the database
Regional distribution of ten key NbS features — circular bar chart showing CBS feature variation by UN world region (ratio within region)
Examples of NbS features in selected cities — nine city-level OSM/GADM map tiles illustrating each coded feature in real urban contexts
Sankey diagram — connections between regions, funding sources, implementing actors, and socio-economic goals among mapped NbS
'Bright spots' for just, transformative NbS — photographic examples from Addu City (Maldives), Durban, São Paulo, and Hamburg
Distribution of capacities for change per city dimension (overall and regional) — most NbS are incremental/reformistic; ecological dimension shows highest transformative share
